enrichment.seo_title: Copysmith Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Copysmith is an AI copywriting platform for e-commerce and marketing teams. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons and FAQs before you buy.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Copysmith is an AI-powered content generation platform designed primarily for e-commerce and marketing teams that need to produce large volumes of on-brand copy quickly. Founded in October 2020 and headquartered in Birmingham, Alabama, the company was originally built around bulk product-description generation for online retailers before expanding into a broader marketing content suite. In October 2022, Copysmith acquired two well-known AI writing tools, Frase (an SEO content research and optimization platform originally based in Boston) and Rytr (a lightweight AI writing assistant), and briefly marketed the combined offering under the name Copyrytr before folding the products back under related branding. As of 2026, Copysmith's product family includes its original bulk copywriting tools, a product data enrichment tool branded Describely for e-commerce catalogs, the Frase SEO content and AI-search-visibility toolkit, and the Rytr multi-channel writing assistant with its MyVoice brand-personalization feature and a Chrome browser extension. The core Copysmith product focuses on generating product titles, descriptions, bullet points, and meta descriptions in bulk, with CSV import and export and a direct Shopify integration so merchandising teams can populate large catalogs without writing every listing by hand. It also includes templates for ad copy, landing pages, email subject lines, and social media captions, tone-of-voice and brand-rule controls to keep output consistent across a team, and a built-in plagiarism checker to screen generated text before publication. Frase adds keyword research, competitor content analysis, an SEO content editor, a site auditor, and tracking of how a site is cited in AI-generated search answers (generative engine optimization). Rytr contributes fast, template-driven writing for shorter-form content and personal brand voice matching. Copysmith is sold on a credit-based subscription model. The Starter plan costs 19 USD per month and includes roughly 75 credits, equivalent to about 40,000 words of generated content per month. The Professional plan costs 59 USD per month (or 590 USD billed annually) and steps up to roughly 400 credits, or about 215,000 words per month, and unlocks additional collaboration and quality-control features. An Enterprise plan is available with custom pricing, negotiated directly with Copysmith's sales team, for organizations that need higher volume, custom integrations, or dedicated support. The company states it serves more than 30,000 content teams and cites large organizations such as Microsoft, Oracle, Coursera, and Target as customers, and reports a 4.8-star rating across roughly 295 or more reviews on G2. Copysmith is best suited for e-commerce merchandising teams that need to generate or refresh thousands of product listings, in-house marketing teams producing high volumes of ad and social copy, and content teams that want SEO research and AI writing bundled into one subscription rather than buying separate point solutions. Because pricing is credit- or word-capped per tier, high-volume publishers may need the Professional or Enterprise plan to avoid running out of monthly credits, and as with any AI writing tool, generated copy generally benefits from human editing for accuracy, originality, and brand nuance before it is published.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Copysmith?
paragraphs: Copysmith is an AI content generation platform built to help e-commerce and marketing teams produce copy at scale rather than one piece at a time. Its original and best-known use case is bulk product content: generating titles, descriptions, bullet points, and meta descriptions for large catalogs, then exporting or pushing that content directly into a store via CSV or a Shopify integration.

Since acquiring Frase and Rytr in 2022, Copysmith has grown into a broader marketing content suite. Frase brings SEO research, content briefs, and an AI-search visibility tracker into the mix, while Rytr contributes fast multi-channel writing templates and a brand-voice personalization feature called MyVoice. Together with Copysmith's own Describely product for e-commerce data enrichment, the platform now spans product copy, SEO articles, ads, email, and social content under one account.

long_form_blocks.heading: Copysmith Pricing Explained
paragraphs: Copysmith uses a credit-based subscription model rather than flat per-seat pricing. The Starter plan is 19 USD per month for about 75 credits, roughly equivalent to 40,000 words of generated content, and covers the core copywriting templates and brand voice settings.

The Professional plan is 59 USD per month, or 590 USD if billed annually, and increases the allowance to roughly 400 credits, about 215,000 words per month, while adding the plagiarism checker and team collaboration features. Organizations that need more volume, custom limits, or dedicated support work with Copysmith's sales team on a custom Enterprise agreement; published self-serve pricing does not extend beyond the Professional tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Copysmith Compares to Alternatives
paragraphs: Copysmith's main differentiator is its e-commerce focus: bulk catalog workflows and Shopify integration are less common in general-purpose AI writing tools like Jasper or Copy.ai, which tend to be built around long-form blog and marketing copy first. Bundling Frase's SEO research and AI-search tracking into the same subscription also sets Copysmith apart from writing-only competitors.

The tradeoff is that credit caps on the entry-level plans can be limiting for very high-volume catalogs or content teams, and because the platform now spans three formerly separate products, some workflows are less unified than a tool built as a single product from the ground up. Teams evaluating Copysmith should weigh its bulk e-commerce strengths against pure-play SEO tools (for keyword-driven articles) or dedicated ad-copy generators (for high-volume paid media).

enrichment.short_description: Core Web Vitals History refers to a category of free, web-based tools that let anyone enter a domain or URL and view how its Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) have changed over time, typically using Google's Chrome UX Report (CrUX) History API. Representative tools include punits.dev's Core Web Vitals Historical viewer, Rumvision's Core Web Vitals History checker, and browser extensions with the same name in the Chrome Web Store.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free to use, no account required
enrichment.seo_title: Core Web Vitals History: Free Tools to Track CrUX Trends
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Core Web Vitals History tools show how a site's LCP, INP and CLS have changed over time using Google's CrUX data. See how they work and where to find them.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Core Web Vitals History is best understood as a category of free, web-based performance tools rather than a single branded product. These tools let a developer, SEO specialist, or site owner enter a domain or specific URL and see a historical chart of that site's Core Web Vitals scores, most commonly the three metrics Google uses for search-relevant page experience signals: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), Interaction to Next Paint (INP), and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS). Rather than a single live lab test, these tools pull from Google's Chrome UX Report (CrUX) History API, which aggregates real-user, field measurement data collected from actual Chrome browser visits to a page over roughly the trailing six months. Because the underlying data source is the same CrUX dataset that powers Google PageSpeed Insights and the Core Web Vitals report in Google Search Console, these history tools are effectively a way to visualize trend lines that would otherwise require manually checking PageSpeed Insights or Search Console week after week and recording the results by hand. Well-known examples of tools carrying this name or serving this purpose include a free checker from Rumvision that shows roughly six months of progress or regression data for a domain, a similarly named viewer at punits.dev built by an independent web performance developer, a CrUX History-based charting tool from TameTheBots, a historical tracker built into the open-source Unlighthouse project that shows 25 weeks of LCP, CLS, and INP trends, and a Chrome Web Store browser extension literally titled Core Web Vitals History. Typical features across these tools include selecting between phone and desktop (mobile versus desktop) real-user data, since CrUX reports the two device categories separately; toggling a chart legend to isolate individual metrics; viewing origin-level data (the whole domain) versus URL-level data (a single page); and exporting or screenshotting the resulting chart for use in an SEO or performance report. A common limitation across all of these tools is that CrUX itself only publishes data for pages and origins that receive sufficient Chrome traffic to meet Google's privacy thresholds, so smaller or lower-traffic sites and pages may show as having insufficient data, especially at the individual URL level rather than the origin level. Because the tools are simple wrappers around a public Google API, they are almost universally offered for free with no account or payment required, and differences between them tend to come down to interface polish, the exact historical window displayed (commonly six months, sometimes up to a year), and whether they present origin data, URL data, or both. For a directory or SEO audience, the practical use case is straightforward: before and after a performance optimization project, a team can use one of these history tools to confirm whether real-world Core Web Vitals actually improved for visitors, rather than relying only on a single synthetic lab test, which does not reflect the diversity of real devices and network conditions that CrUX field data captures. This makes Core Web Vitals History tools a useful complement to lab-based testing tools such as Lighthouse or PageSpeed Insights, which measure a single simulated page load rather than aggregated real-user experience over time.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Are Core Web Vitals History Tools?
paragraphs: Core Web Vitals History tools are free, web-based utilities that chart how a website's Core Web Vitals metrics, Largest Contentful Paint, Interaction to Next Paint, and Cumulative Layout Shift, have trended over recent months. Instead of a single point-in-time score, they pull from Google's Chrome UX Report (CrUX) History API, which stores rolling real-user measurement data collected from actual Chrome browser visits.

Because this is the same field data that underlies Google PageSpeed Insights and the Core Web Vitals report in Google Search Console, these history tools essentially save site owners from manually checking those reports over and over to spot trends. Several tools use this exact naming or purpose, including checkers from Rumvision and punits.dev, a charting feature inside the open-source Unlighthouse project, and a Chrome Web Store browser extension.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Core Web Vitals History Tools Source Their Data
paragraphs: These tools query Google's public CrUX History API, which typically returns roughly six months of monthly or weekly snapshots for a given origin or URL, broken out by device type (phone versus desktop) and, in more detailed implementations, connection type.

Because CrUX only publishes data for pages that receive enough Chrome traffic to meet Google's aggregation and privacy thresholds, lower-traffic pages, and sometimes even entire smaller sites, may show as having insufficient historical data, particularly at the individual-URL level rather than the whole-domain (origin) level.

long_form_blocks.heading: When to Use History Tools Instead of Lab Tests
paragraphs: Lab-based tools such as Lighthouse or a single PageSpeed Insights run measure one simulated page load under controlled conditions, which is useful for debugging but does not represent the range of real devices and networks actual visitors use.

Core Web Vitals History tools are better suited to answering a different question: did a specific optimization, deployment, or redesign measurably improve (or regress) real-user experience over the following weeks or months. Many teams use both together, lab tools for diagnosis and history tools for verification.

faqs.question: What does a Core Web Vitals History tool actually measure?
faqs.answer: It charts a website's historical Largest Contentful Paint, Interaction to Next Paint, and Cumulative Layout Shift scores using Google's CrUX (Chrome UX Report) field data, typically covering the trailing six months.

faqs.question: Is Core Web Vitals History data the same as PageSpeed Insights?
faqs.answer: It draws from the same underlying CrUX dataset that powers PageSpeed Insights and Google Search Console's Core Web Vitals report, but presents it as a historical trend chart rather than a single current score.

faqs.question: Are Core Web Vitals History tools free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the tools in this category, including implementations from Rumvision, punits.dev, TameTheBots, Unlighthouse, and a Chrome Web Store extension, are free to use.

faqs.question: Why does my site show no historical Core Web Vitals data?
faqs.answer: Google's CrUX dataset only reports data for origins and pages that receive enough real Chrome traffic to meet its privacy and aggregation thresholds; lower-traffic sites or individual pages often will not have published data.

faqs.question: How far back does Core Web Vitals History data go?
faqs.answer: Most implementations show roughly six months of history, since that is the typical window available through the CrUX History API, though some tools extend coverage further.

faqs.question: Does Core Web Vitals History separate mobile and desktop results?
faqs.answer: Yes, CrUX reports phone and desktop data separately, and most history tools let users toggle between the two device categories.

faqs.question: Should I use a Core Web Vitals History tool instead of Lighthouse?
faqs.answer: They serve different purposes: Lighthouse gives a detailed lab diagnosis of a single page load, while history tools show whether real visitors actually experienced better performance over time, so they work best used together.

faqs.question: Can I check a single page instead of a whole domain?
faqs.answer: Some tools support URL-level checks in addition to origin-level (whole-domain) checks, though URL-level data is less consistently available due to CrUX's traffic thresholds.

Founded in 2023 and based in San Francisco, it is backed by Y Combinator and positions itself as an AI-native alternative to traditional static application security testing (SAST) tools.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Corgea is an AI-native application security platform that finds and auto-fixes vulnerabilities. Compare 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Corgea is an AI-powered application security (AppSec) platform that scans source code, open-source dependencies, containers, and infrastructure-as-code configurations to detect vulnerabilities and then uses AI agents to generate safe, ready-to-apply code fixes. The company was founded in 2023, is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and is backed by Y Combinator; public reporting places its team at roughly six employees as of research in 2026, making it a small, focused startup rather than a large incumbent security vendor. Corgea markets itself as an AI-native alternative to traditional static application security testing (SAST) tools, arguing that legacy SAST products generate large volumes of findings that developers do not have time to triage or fix manually, and that fixing a single vulnerability conventionally costs somewhere between 400 and 4,000 US dollars in developer time. Corgea's core pitch is that its AI agents can validate which findings are real (reducing false positives), triage them by severity and exploitability, and then draft the actual code fix inside the developer's existing workflow, cutting remediation time and cost significantly compared to manual fixing. The platform integrates with GitHub, GitLab, Azure DevOps, and Bitbucket, and can run as scheduled repository scans or as pull-request scans that block or flag risky changes before merge. Beyond classic SAST-style pattern scanning, Corgea also covers logic and authentication/authorization vulnerability detection, software composition analysis (dependency scanning) for vulnerable open-source packages, secrets detection for accidentally committed credentials, container image scanning, and infrastructure-as-code scanning for cloud misconfigurations. Corgea's pricing is structured around four tiers. The Free plan supports a team of up to two members, up to ten repositories, and up to ten pull-request scans per month, and includes the core AI SAST engine plus logic/auth scanning, dependency scanning, secrets detection, container scanning, and infrastructure-as-code scanning, with community forum support. The Growth plan costs 39 US dollars per developer per month with a minimum of five developers, raises the repository limit to 100 with unlimited PR scans, and adds full pull-request scanning, a code-quality layer, the Corgea Agent for automated fix generation, a Jira integration, software license enforcement, and up to 50 AI-generated auto-fixes. The Scale plan, described by Corgea as its most popular tier, costs 49 US dollars per developer per month with a minimum of 20 developers, raises the repository limit to 200, and adds custom and blocking rules, reporting and analytics dashboards, team management controls, APIs and webhooks, and up to 200 auto-fixes. The Enterprise plan is custom-priced, removes team and repository limits, and adds SSO and SCIM provisioning, single-tenant deployment options, formal SLA management, audit logging, and premium support. All paid tiers support both monthly and annual billing. Customer references include Epilot, a European utilities and energy software platform, which has publicly described rolling out Corgea's native GitLab integration to its engineering team in under a week, without the enterprise-only pricing or added CI complexity it experienced with a prior AppSec vendor. Corgea is best suited for engineering and security teams that want vulnerability findings paired directly with an actionable, AI-drafted fix rather than a raw list of issues to investigate manually, particularly smaller and mid-sized engineering organizations that lack a dedicated large application security team.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Corgea?
paragraphs: Corgea is an AI-native application security platform that goes beyond traditional static analysis by pairing vulnerability detection with AI-generated code fixes. Rather than only flagging a list of potential issues for a developer to research and patch manually, Corgea's AI agents validate findings, triage them, and draft a proposed fix that can be applied directly in the existing pull-request workflow.

Founded in 2023 and based in San Francisco, Corgea is a Y Combinator-backed startup with a small team, positioning itself against larger, more established SAST vendors by emphasizing faster setup, lower false-positive rates, and automated remediation rather than detection alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Corgea Scans and Detects
paragraphs: Corgea's coverage spans several categories of application risk in one platform: classic static code vulnerabilities (AI SAST), business logic and authentication/authorization flaws that pattern-matching tools often miss, software composition analysis for vulnerable third-party dependencies, secrets detection for leaked credentials or API keys committed to a repository, container image scanning, and infrastructure-as-code scanning for cloud configuration risks.

It integrates with the major source control platforms, GitHub, GitLab, Azure DevOps, and Bitbucket, and can run as scheduled full-repository scans or as pull-request scans that surface and can optionally block risky changes before they merge.

long_form_blocks.heading: Corgea Pricing Tiers Explained
paragraphs: Corgea offers a genuinely free tier (up to two team members, ten repositories, and ten PR scans per month) that includes its full scanning engine, making it accessible for small teams or evaluation before committing to a paid plan.

Paid plans are priced per developer per month: Growth at 39 USD (minimum five developers) adds the Corgea Agent for automated fixes and Jira integration, while Scale at 49 USD (minimum 20 developers) adds custom rules, analytics, and team management. Enterprise is custom-priced and adds SSO/SCIM, single-tenant deployment, and formal SLAs for larger, compliance-driven organizations.

faqs.question: Is Corgea free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Corgea offers a free plan for up to two team members, ten repositories, and ten pull-request scans per month, including its core AI SAST, logic/auth, dependency, secrets, container, and infrastructure-as-code scanning.

faqs.question: How much does Corgea cost on paid plans?
faqs.answer: The Growth plan is 39 USD per developer per month with a minimum of five developers, and the Scale plan is 49 USD per developer per month with a minimum of 20 developers; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Does Corgea automatically fix vulnerabilities?
faqs.answer: Corgea's paid plans include the Corgea Agent, which generates AI-drafted code fixes for detected vulnerabilities that developers can review and apply within their existing pull-request workflow, up to a monthly auto-fix quota per plan.

faqs.question: Which source control platforms does Corgea support?
faqs.answer: Corgea integrates natively with GitHub, GitLab, Azure DevOps, and Bitbucket for both scheduled repository scans and pull-request scanning.

faqs.question: Who founded Corgea and where is it based?
faqs.answer: Corgea was founded in 2023 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California; it is backed by Y Combinator.

faqs.question: Does Corgea replace traditional SAST tools?
faqs.answer: Corgea positions itself as an AI-native alternative to traditional SAST tools, aiming to reduce false positives and pair each finding with an actionable fix rather than only a list of flagged issues.

faqs.question: Does Corgea support enterprise security requirements like SSO?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Enterprise plan adds SSO and SCIM provisioning, single-tenant deployment, audit logging, and formal SLA management for larger or compliance-driven organizations.

enrichment.short_description: CORS-Tester refers to a category of free, web-based utilities that let developers check whether an API or web endpoint's Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) configuration is set up correctly. Representative tools include TestingBot's CORS Tester, Corsfix's CORS Tester, EaseCloud's CORS Tester, and CodeHappy's CORS Tester, which send preflight and actual cross-origin requests and report back the resulting CORS headers.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free to use, no account required
enrichment.seo_title: CORS Tester: Free Tools to Check CORS Headers and Config
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CORS Tester tools let you check if an API or site has valid CORS headers by simulating cross-origin requests. See how they work and where to find them.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CORS-Tester describes a category of free, browser-based diagnostic tools built to help developers verify whether a web server or API has Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) configured correctly. CORS is a browser security mechanism that restricts whether JavaScript running on one origin, meaning a specific combination of scheme, domain, and port, is allowed to make requests to a different origin. When a frontend application hosted on one domain tries to call an API hosted on another domain, the browser first checks the CORS headers the server returns before allowing the request to complete, and misconfigured CORS is one of the most common integration problems developers encounter when connecting a frontend to a backend API, particularly during local development or when adding a new client to an existing service. A CORS-Tester tool automates the process of checking this configuration without requiring the developer to write test code or dig through browser developer console errors. Typically, the user enters a target URL and specifies the origin that should be allowed to call it, along with the HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, and so on) and any custom headers the real request would send. The tool then issues the same preflight OPTIONS request and, in many implementations, the actual follow-up request that a browser would send, and reports back the CORS-related response headers, such as Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, Access-Control-Allow-Headers, and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials, along with a pass or fail summary and often plain-language explanations of what is misconfigured. Several independent tools carry this name or serve this exact purpose. TestingBot offers a free online CORS Tester as part of its browser and API testing suite. Corsfix, a company focused on CORS proxy and debugging tooling, offers a CORS Tester that checks any URL for CORS configuration issues at no cost. EaseCloud provides a CORS Tester within its broader collection of free API and network tools. CodeHappy's CORS Tester allows testing by HTTP method and displays detailed header information along with troubleshooting tips. Other implementations include ToolDock's CORS Tester, which runs both the preflight and actual request from its own server and returns a plain-English pass or fail analysis, TestMu AI's (formerly LambdaTest) free CORS Tester, WebBrowserTools' Test CORS utility, and U2Tool's CORS Tester. All of these tools are free to use, require no registration, and are designed as quick diagnostic utilities rather than full API testing platforms. For a directory or SEO context, the practical value of these tools is straightforward: rather than reproducing a CORS error by trial and error inside a browser and reading raw console output, a developer can paste an endpoint and origin into one of these testers and get an immediate, structured answer about which specific CORS header is missing, misconfigured, or too permissive (a common security issue when an API returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin as a wildcard alongside credentialed requests). This makes CORS-Tester tools a common step in both frontend-backend integration debugging and basic API security review workflows, and they are frequently bookmarked by web developers alongside other free diagnostic utilities like DNS lookup, SSL checker, and HTTP header inspection tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is a CORS Tester?
paragraphs: A CORS Tester is a free, web-based diagnostic tool that checks whether a server or API has Cross-Origin Resource Sharing configured correctly for a given origin, HTTP method, and set of headers. It automates the same preflight and actual request checks a browser performs, then reports the resulting CORS response headers in a readable format.

CORS-Tester is not one single branded product; it describes a category served by multiple independent tools, including offerings from TestingBot, Corsfix, EaseCloud, CodeHappy, ToolDock, TestMu AI (formerly LambdaTest), WebBrowserTools, and U2Tool, all of which are free and require no account.

long_form_blocks.heading: How CORS Testing Tools Work
paragraphs: A typical CORS tester asks for a target URL, the origin that should be allowed to call it, the HTTP method the real request would use, and any custom headers the request would send. It then issues an OPTIONS preflight request, and often the actual follow-up request, exactly as a browser would.

The tool reads back headers such as Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, Access-Control-Allow-Headers, and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials, then compares them against what the browser would require to allow the request, returning a pass or fail result with an explanation of any mismatch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Common CORS Issues These Tools Catch
paragraphs: The most frequent issue these tools surface is a missing or incorrect Access-Control-Allow-Origin header, where the server either omits the header entirely or lists an origin that does not match the calling frontend's domain.

Other common findings include a server that allows a wildcard origin while also trying to allow credentialed requests, which browsers block outright, missing methods in Access-Control-Allow-Methods for non-GET requests, and custom request headers that are not included in Access-Control-Allow-Headers, all of which a CORS tester can identify without the developer needing to reproduce the error manually in a browser.

features.description: Sends an OPTIONS preflight request to the target URL exactly as a browser would before a cross-origin call.

features.title: Actual Request Testing
features.description: In many implementations, also sends the real follow-up request to confirm the server's actual CORS behavior, not just the preflight response.

features.title: Custom Origin and Method Selection
features.description: Lets the user specify the calling origin and HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, and others) to test against.

features.title: CORS Header Breakdown
features.description: Displays the specific CORS response headers returned, such as Access-Control-Allow-Origin, -Methods, -Headers, and -Credentials.

features.title: Pass or Fail Summary
features.description: Provides a clear pass/fail result with a plain-language explanation of what is misconfigured, if anything.

features.title: Custom Header Testing
features.description: Allows adding custom request headers to test whether the server correctly allows them for cross-origin calls.

features.title: No Account Required
features.description: Most implementations are free single-page tools requiring no sign-up or installation.

features.title: Server-Side Request Execution
features.description: Runs the test request from the tool's own server rather than the user's browser, avoiding local browser CORS restrictions from interfering with the test itself.

pros: Free to use with no account or payment required across essentially all known implementations
Faster and clearer than manually reproducing CORS errors in a browser console
Multiple independent tools exist, giving developers a choice of interface and feature depth
Useful both for frontend-backend integration debugging and for basic API security review
Server-side execution avoids the calling browser's own CORS restrictions interfering with the test
No installation or setup required beyond entering a URL
cons: As a category rather than one product, feature depth, accuracy, and reliability vary between tools
Some implementations only test the preflight OPTIONS request and not the actual follow-up request
Results reflect the server's configuration at test time and will not catch intermittent or environment-specific misconfigurations
Free tools may have rate limits or lack support for authenticated endpoints requiring cookies or tokens
Not a substitute for a full security review of an API's authentication and authorization model
Some tools may not clearly explain wildcard-plus-credentials misconfigurations, which are a common but subtle security risk

faqs.question: What does a CORS Tester check?
faqs.answer: It checks whether a target URL returns the correct Cross-Origin Resource Sharing headers, such as Access-Control-Allow-Origin, -Methods, -Headers, and -Credentials, for a given calling origin, HTTP method, and set of request headers.

faqs.question: Are CORS Tester tools free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the known implementations, including those from TestingBot, Corsfix, EaseCloud, CodeHappy, ToolDock, TestMu AI, WebBrowserTools, and U2Tool, are free to use with no registration required.

faqs.question: How do I use a CORS Tester?
faqs.answer: Enter the target URL in the endpoint field, set the origin to the domain your frontend runs on, select the HTTP method you plan to use, optionally add custom headers, then run the test to see whether the request passes or fails and why.

faqs.question: Why is CORS blocking my API requests?
faqs.answer: CORS blocks a cross-origin request when the server's response does not include an Access-Control-Allow-Origin header matching the calling origin, or is missing required allowed methods or headers; a CORS Tester identifies exactly which header is the problem.

faqs.question: Can a CORS Tester test authenticated endpoints?
faqs.answer: Support varies by tool; many free CORS testers are built for simple public endpoint checks and may have limited support for cookies, tokens, or other authenticated request scenarios.

faqs.question: Is a wildcard Access-Control-Allow-Origin a security risk?
faqs.answer: It can be, particularly when combined with credentialed requests (cookies or authorization headers), which browsers block by design; several CORS testers flag this specific misconfiguration.

faqs.question: Do CORS Tester tools run from my browser or their server?
faqs.answer: Most run the test request from the tool's own server rather than the user's browser, which avoids the browser's own CORS restrictions interfering with the diagnostic test itself.

faqs.question: Is CORS Tester a single company's product?
faqs.answer: No, it describes a category of similar free tools built by different companies and individual developers, rather than one single branded product.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cosmic is an AI-powered headless CMS with a content API, TypeScript SDK, and built-in AI agents. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cosmic is a headless content management system built for teams that want a flexible, API-first content backend combined with built-in artificial intelligence features. The company was started by co-founders Carson Gibbons and Tony Spiro, with the idea for the product taking shape around 2014 and a public launch of an early version around 2016; Cosmic later went through Y Combinator's Winter 2019 (W19) batch, and Tony Spiro serves as CEO. The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and, according to available reporting, operates with a small team of roughly eight people, which is typical for a venture-backed, product-led developer tools startup at its stage. As a headless CMS, Cosmic separates content management from content presentation: editors manage structured content, images, and metadata inside a web dashboard, and developers pull that content into any frontend framework or channel, website, mobile app, or other digital product, through a REST API that Cosmic advertises as returning responses in under 100 milliseconds. Developer tooling includes a TypeScript SDK, a command-line interface (CLI), and, reflecting a broader industry shift toward AI-assisted development, a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that plugs directly into AI coding assistants such as Cursor, Claude Code, and GitHub Copilot, letting developers query and modify Cosmic content as part of an AI-assisted coding workflow. Cosmic's most distinctive positioning in 2026 is around AI agents built into the CMS itself, rather than as a bolt-on feature. The company describes several agent types: a Team Agent that lives inside messaging tools such as Slack, WhatsApp, or Telegram, where a user defines its persona and goal and it maintains persistent conversation memory while triggering actions in the CMS, the codebase, or third-party APIs from natural-language instructions; Content Agents that can publish and manage content autonomously; and Code Agents described as able to ship features and fix bugs. On the content-modeling side, Cosmic supports more than 20 metafield types for structuring content, AI-assisted image and video generation for media assets, and a design choice the company markets as no migrations ever, meaning content model changes do not require the kind of database migration scripts common in some other CMS or database-backed systems. Cosmic's content containers are called Buckets, and pricing scales primarily by the number of Buckets, team members, and content Objects included in each plan. The Free plan costs 0 USD per month and includes 1 Bucket, 2 team members, and up to 1,000 Objects, with no credit card required to start. The Builder plan is 49 USD per month and includes 2 Buckets, 3 team members, and up to 5,000 Objects. The Team plan is 299 USD per month and includes 3 Buckets, 5 team members, and up to 20,000 Objects. The Business plan is 499 USD per month and includes 5 Buckets, 10 team members, and up to 50,000 Objects. Across paid plans, additional team members beyond the included allotment can be added for 29 USD per user per month. Cosmic is best suited for development teams building content-driven websites, apps, or SaaS products who want an API-first CMS rather than a traditional monolithic content platform, and particularly for teams interested in experimenting with AI agents that can manage or publish content with minimal manual intervention, or with integrating their CMS content directly into AI-assisted coding tools via the MCP server.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Cosmic?
paragraphs: Cosmic is a headless content management system, meaning content is managed through a dashboard and delivered to any frontend, website, app, or other channel, through an API rather than a tightly coupled templating system. It targets developers who want a flexible content backend and content teams who want an intuitive editing interface without needing developer help for every content change.

Co-founded by Carson Gibbons and Tony Spiro, with the underlying idea originating around 2014 and an early public version launching around 2016, Cosmic went through Y Combinator's W19 batch and is headquartered in San Francisco. Tony Spiro serves as CEO of the roughly eight-person team as of available reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cosmic's Built-In AI Agents
paragraphs: Cosmic differentiates itself from most headless CMS competitors by building AI agents directly into the platform rather than offering AI only as a writing-assist feature. Its Team Agent operates inside messaging tools like Slack, WhatsApp, or Telegram, maintaining conversation memory and triggering actions in the CMS, connected codebases, or third-party APIs based on natural-language requests.

The company also describes Content Agents that can publish and manage content and Code Agents aimed at shipping features and fixing bugs, positioning Cosmic as what it calls an agentic CMS. A Model Context Protocol (MCP) server extends this further by letting AI coding assistants such as Cursor, Claude Code, and GitHub Copilot interact directly with a project's Cosmic content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cosmic Pricing Explained
paragraphs: Cosmic uses a Bucket-based pricing model, where a Bucket is a content container, combined with limits on team members and content Objects. The Free plan (0 USD) includes 1 Bucket, 2 team members, and 1,000 Objects, and requires no credit card, making it usable for small projects or evaluation.

Paid plans scale from Builder at 49 USD per month (2 Buckets, 3 members, 5,000 Objects) through Team at 299 USD per month (3 Buckets, 5 members, 20,000 Objects) to Business at 499 USD per month (5 Buckets, 10 members, 50,000 Objects), with additional team members available for 29 USD per user per month on any paid plan.

It combines key-value, SQL++ query, full-text and vector search, analytics, eventing, and mobile sync services in one managed operational database platform aimed partly at AI application workloads.

enrichment.seo_title: Couchbase Capella Review 2026: Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Couchbase Capella is Couchbase's fully managed NoSQL DBaaS. Compare 2026 pricing tiers, features, pros, cons and FAQs for this cloud database platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Couchbase Capella is the fully managed Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS) product from Couchbase, Inc., built on top of the company's underlying Couchbase NoSQL database engine. Couchbase, Inc. was formed in February 2011 through the merger of two earlier companies, Membase (originally founded as NorthScale in 2009) and CouchOne, and the combined company built a distributed, JSON document database that blends key-value performance with SQL-like querying. Couchbase Cloud, the predecessor to Capella, reached general availability on AWS in July 2020, and the product was rebranded and relaunched as Capella around October and November of 2021 before expanding to Google Cloud in June 2022 and Microsoft Azure in January 2023, giving it managed availability across all three major public clouds. Couchbase is headquartered in San Jose, California, with additional offices in locations including Austin, Bengaluru, Tel Aviv, Singapore, and London, and reported approximately 891 employees as of research conducted in 2026. In September 2025, Couchbase was acquired by Haveli Investments in a deal valued at approximately 1.5 billion US dollars, moving the company from public ownership to private equity ownership. Capella packages together several database services under one managed platform: SQL++ (a SQL-compatible query language for JSON documents, sometimes marketed through an AI assistant called Capella iQ), traditional key-value access for low-latency lookups, full-text and vector search for building AI and semantic search applications, an analytics service for operational reporting without impacting transactional workloads, an eventing service for reacting to data changes in real time, and Mobile App Services for syncing data to edge and mobile devices with offline support. Access control is managed through role-based access control (RBAC) scoped to specific data scopes and collections, and clusters support cross data center replication (XDCR) for multi-region deployments starting at the Basic tier. Capella pricing is consumption-based, structured around a credit system rather than flat monthly fees. There is a genuine Free tier with no listed cost, limited to a single node with 8GB of capacity, sample datasets, and community forum support only, suitable for prototyping and evaluation. Above that, Couchbase defines a Basic tier billed at approximately 1.00 USD per Capella Basic Credit and starting around 0.15 USD per node-hour, offering a single-cluster availability zone, 24-hour backup intervals, cross data center replication, and community forum support with a 99.5 percent uptime SLA on multi-node clusters. A Developer Pro tier is billed at approximately 1.25 USD per Capella Developer Pro Credit and starts around 0.35 USD per node-hour, requiring a minimum of three nodes across multiple availability zones, offering backups as frequent as every hour, 8-hour 24x7 support response, and a 99.99 percent uptime SLA. An Enterprise tier is billed at approximately 1.75 USD per Capella Enterprise Credit and starts around 0.49 USD per node-hour, also requiring a minimum three-node cluster, and adds customer-managed encryption keys via KMIP, cluster and App Services auditing, and premium 30-minute 24x7 support response with the same 99.99 percent uptime SLA. Because pricing is consumption-based and varies by node size, region, and cloud provider, actual monthly costs depend heavily on workload and configuration rather than a single flat number. Capella is best suited for engineering teams that need a distributed, horizontally scalable NoSQL database with flexible JSON schema, built-in caching-tier performance, and increasingly, native vector search for retrieval-augmented generation and other AI application patterns, without operating the underlying Couchbase clusters themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Couchbase Capella?
paragraphs: Couchbase Capella is Couchbase's fully managed Database-as-a-Service offering, built on the company's distributed NoSQL database engine that combines key-value performance with SQL-like querying over JSON documents. Rather than self-hosting and operating Couchbase Server clusters, teams provision managed clusters through Capella on AWS, Google Cloud, or Microsoft Azure.

Couchbase, Inc. traces back to a February 2011 merger of Membase (originally NorthScale, founded 2009) and CouchOne. The managed cloud product launched as Couchbase Cloud in 2020, was rebranded Capella in late 2021, and expanded across all three major public clouds by early 2023. The company is headquartered in San Jose, California, and was acquired by Haveli Investments in September 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Capella's Database Services
paragraphs: Capella bundles multiple data services on top of its core document database: SQL++ for SQL-compatible querying of JSON data, key-value access for low-latency lookups, full-text and vector search for building semantic and AI-driven search and retrieval features, an analytics service for reporting workloads isolated from transactional traffic, an eventing service for real-time reactions to data changes, and Mobile App Services for syncing data to edge and mobile clients with offline support.

Clusters support role-based access control scoped to specific data scopes and collections, and, starting at the Basic paid tier, cross data center replication (XDCR) for multi-region resilience, along with scheduled backups whose frequency increases at higher tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Capella Pricing Works
paragraphs: Capella uses a consumption-based, credit-driven pricing model rather than flat monthly plans. A Free tier offers a single 8GB node with community support for prototyping at no cost, while paid usage is billed per node-hour, converted into tier-specific credits: roughly 1.00 USD per Basic credit, 1.25 USD per Developer Pro credit, and 1.75 USD per Enterprise credit.

Node-hour starting rates are approximately 0.15 USD (Basic), 0.35 USD (Developer Pro), and 0.49 USD (Enterprise), with higher tiers requiring larger minimum cluster sizes and adding features like more frequent backups, higher uptime SLAs, customer-managed encryption keys, and premium support response times. Actual costs scale with node count, node size, cloud region, and workload.

faqs.question: What is Couchbase Capella?
faqs.answer: Couchbase Capella is the fully managed Database-as-a-Service version of Couchbase's NoSQL database, available on AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure, bundling key-value, document, search, analytics, eventing, and mobile sync services.

faqs.question: How is Couchbase Capella priced?
faqs.answer: Capella uses consumption-based, credit-driven pricing billed per node-hour, with a free tier plus Basic, Developer Pro, and Enterprise tiers starting at approximately 0.15, 0.35, and 0.49 USD per node-hour respectively.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Couchbase Capella?
faqs.answer: Yes, Capella offers a Free tier limited to a single node with 8GB capacity, sample data, and community forum support, suitable for prototyping rather than production.

faqs.question: Does Couchbase Capella support vector search for AI applications?

enrichment.short_description: Apache CouchDB is a free, open-source NoSQL document database that stores data as JSON, uses JavaScript for MapReduce views, and exposes an HTTP/REST API for all operations. Created in 2005 and governed since 2008 as a top-level Apache Software Foundation project, it is best known for its multi-master replication protocol, which underpins many offline-first mobile and edge applications.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (Apache License 2.0); no paid tiers from the Apache Software Foundation
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable, governed by a volunteer community under the Apache Software Foundation

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apache CouchDB is a free, open-source NoSQL document database known for HTTP APIs and multi-master replication. See features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apache CouchDB is a free, open-source NoSQL document-oriented database originally created in April 2005 by Damien Katz, a former IBM Lotus Notes developer, who self-funded and developed the project independently for close to two years before releasing it publicly under an open-source license. In February 2008, CouchDB was accepted as an Apache Incubator project and relicensed under the Apache License, and it graduated to a top-level Apache Software Foundation (ASF) project a few months later. As an ASF project, CouchDB has no company behind it and no traditional headquarters; it is governed by a self-selected group of active contributors following Apache's standard open-source governance model, with the Apache Software Foundation itself legally registered in Wilmington, Delaware, in the United States, though contributors and users are distributed globally. CouchDB is implemented in Erlang, a language chosen specifically for its strengths in building fault-tolerant, concurrent, distributed systems, which aligns with CouchDB's core design goals around reliability and replication. Data is stored as JSON documents, queries are historically expressed as JavaScript-based MapReduce views (with Mango, a more developer-friendly, MongoDB-inspired declarative query language, added in version 2.0), and every database operation, from reading and writing documents to managing replication and administration, is exposed through an HTTP/REST API, meaning CouchDB can be queried directly with a web browser or standard HTTP tooling without a dedicated client driver. This HTTP-native design is one of CouchDB's most distinctive characteristics relative to other NoSQL databases of its era. CouchDB's other defining feature is its replication protocol, which supports multi-master (bidirectional) replication between any two CouchDB instances, or between CouchDB and compatible systems, allowing changes made offline on one node to sync and merge automatically once connectivity is restored, with document-level conflict detection when the same document is modified concurrently on multiple nodes. This replication model made CouchDB an early and influential foundation for a category of applications now generally described as offline-first, particularly mobile apps and applications operating in environments with unreliable or intermittent network connectivity; the CouchDB replication protocol also underpins related projects such as PouchDB, a JavaScript database designed to sync with CouchDB (and CouchDB-compatible servers) from inside a web browser. CouchDB includes a built-in web-based administration interface called Fauxton for managing databases, documents, and configuration without needing a separate client application. Because CouchDB is released under the Apache License 2.0 and distributed by the Apache Software Foundation, it is entirely free to download, self-host, and modify, with no paid tiers, licensing fees, or vendor lock-in from the ASF itself; commercial support and managed hosting are instead available from various third-party companies and cloud providers rather than from Apache directly. CouchDB is best suited for teams building offline-capable mobile or edge applications that need reliable bidirectional sync, teams that want a document database queryable directly over HTTP without a specialized client library, and organizations that prefer a fully open-source database with no vendor licensing costs, in exchange for taking on the operational responsibility of self-hosting, scaling, and maintaining the database themselves, since there is no official ASF-run managed cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: The History of Apache CouchDB
paragraphs: CouchDB was created in April 2005 by Damien Katz, a former IBM Lotus Notes developer, who self-funded development of the project for nearly two years before releasing it as open source. In February 2008, the project entered the Apache Incubator and was relicensed under the Apache License, graduating to a top-level Apache Software Foundation project a few months later.

As an Apache project, CouchDB has no owning company or corporate headquarters; it is maintained by a self-selected community of active contributors under the ASF's standard open-source governance model. The Apache Software Foundation itself is legally registered in Wilmington, Delaware, though CouchDB's actual contributors and users are distributed around the world.

long_form_blocks.heading: CouchDB's Architecture and HTTP API
paragraphs: CouchDB is implemented in Erlang, a language built for fault-tolerant, highly concurrent, distributed systems, which supports CouchDB's design priorities around reliability and replication. Data is stored as JSON documents, and CouchDB exposes every operation, reads, writes, replication, and administration, through an HTTP/REST API, meaning a database can be queried using nothing more than standard HTTP tooling or even a browser.

Querying was originally handled through JavaScript-based MapReduce views; version 2.0 added Mango, a more approachable, MongoDB-inspired declarative query language, alongside significant performance improvements to replication and compaction and a rewritten Fauxton administration interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Replication and Offline-First Applications
paragraphs: CouchDB's most distinctive feature is its replication protocol, which supports multi-master (bidirectional) sync between any two compatible CouchDB instances, with automatic document-level conflict detection when the same document changes on multiple nodes independently.

This replication model is the foundation for a wide range of offline-first applications, particularly on mobile and in environments with unreliable connectivity, and it also underlies related projects such as PouchDB, a JavaScript database built to sync with CouchDB-compatible servers directly from within a web browser.

faqs.question: Is Apache CouchDB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CouchDB is completely free and open source, released under the Apache License 2.0 by the Apache Software Foundation, with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: Who created CouchDB and when?
faqs.answer: CouchDB was created in April 2005 by Damien Katz, a former IBM Lotus Notes developer, and became an Apache Incubator project in February 2008 before graduating to a top-level Apache Software Foundation project.

faqs.question: What programming language is CouchDB written in?
faqs.answer: CouchDB is implemented in Erlang, a language designed for building fault-tolerant, highly concurrent, distributed systems.

faqs.question: What makes CouchDB different from other NoSQL databases?
faqs.answer: CouchDB is distinguished by exposing all database operations through an HTTP/REST API and by its multi-master replication protocol, which supports offline-first applications with automatic document-level conflict detection.

faqs.question: Does CouchDB have a managed cloud hosting option?
faqs.answer: The Apache Software Foundation does not offer an official managed cloud service for CouchDB; managed hosting and commercial support are available from various third-party providers instead.

faqs.question: What query language does CouchDB use?
faqs.answer: CouchDB originally used JavaScript-based MapReduce views for querying and indexing, and added Mango, a more approachable declarative, MongoDB-inspired query language, starting in version 2.0.

faqs.question: What is Fauxton in CouchDB?
faqs.answer: Fauxton is CouchDB's built-in, web-based administration interface for managing databases, documents, and server configuration without a separate client application.

faqs.question: Is CouchDB good for offline-first mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, CouchDB's multi-master replication protocol is a common foundation for offline-first applications, and it underlies related projects such as PouchDB, which syncs directly with CouchDB-compatible servers from within a browser.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Country-State-City Microservice API describes a category of open-source and free-tier services that give developers structured, hierarchical geographic data, countries, their states or provinces, and their cities, for use in address forms, shipping calculators, location-based autocomplete fields, and similar geographic UI components. Rather than a single company-owned product, this space is populated by a mix of hosted APIs and open-source datasets that developers can self-host as a lightweight microservice. The best-known hosted option, countrystatecity.in, describes itself as covering more than 250 countries, more than 5,300 states, and more than 153,800 cities, delivered through both REST and GraphQL endpoints with sub-200ms response times and a stated 99.9 percent uptime target. The service supports exporting data in CSV, JSON, or SQL formats, offers a free tier to start building, and reports being used by more than 50,000 developers with over 5.1 billion cumulative API requests processed. Its underlying data is described as ODbL-1.0 licensed for the core geographic database, with an MIT license applied to its accompanying software packages, and the dataset is also distributed via NPM, PyPI, and a command-line interface for teams that prefer to self-host rather than call a hosted API. On the open-source side, the most widely referenced underlying dataset in this category is the countries-states-cities-database project, maintained on GitHub, which compiles country, state, and city records into JSON, SQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, MongoDB, SQLite, XML, YAML, and CSV formats, including ISO2 and ISO3 country codes, country calling codes, capitals, native language names, and time zone information for countries. Because this dataset is open source, multiple independent developers have built their own microservice wrappers and APIs on top of it or similar datasets, including Laravel-based REST APIs, autocomplete-focused services, and, more recently, packages that ship as both an NPM library and a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server so that AI coding assistants and agents can query geographic data directly. Typical features across implementations in this category include full country lists with ISO codes, states or provinces nested under each country, cities nested under each state, search and autocomplete endpoints, filtering by country or state, and multiple export or delivery formats. Because much of the underlying data is community-maintained and periodically synced from public sources, hosted services in this category commonly describe their processes as involving community-driven updates, automated syncing, and real-time data validation, though the precision and completeness of city-level data can vary meaningfully by country, particularly for smaller administrative divisions or less-documented regions. For a directory or SEO context, the practical appeal of tools in this category is that they let developers avoid manually maintaining their own country, state, and city reference tables, a common but tedious requirement for e-commerce checkout flows, user registration forms, shipping and tax calculators, and any application that needs structured, hierarchical location data without building and updating that dataset in-house. Because most implementations offer either a genuinely free tier or a fully open-source, self-hostable dataset, the barrier to adoption is low, and teams often choose between a hosted API for convenience or a self-hosted open-source dataset for full control over data freshness, licensing, and infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is a Country-State-City Microservice API?
paragraphs: A Country-State-City Microservice API is a service, hosted or self-hosted, that provides structured, hierarchical geographic reference data: a list of countries, the states or provinces within each country, and the cities within each state. Developers use this data to power address forms, shipping calculators, and location autocomplete fields without maintaining the underlying dataset themselves.

This is a category populated by multiple independent projects rather than a single product. Hosted services such as countrystatecity.in offer REST and GraphQL APIs with a free tier, while open-source datasets such as the countries-states-cities-database project on GitHub let teams self-host the same kind of data as a lightweight internal microservice.

long_form_blocks.heading: How the Underlying Data Is Sourced and Licensed
paragraphs: Hosted implementations like countrystatecity.in describe their core geographic database as licensed under ODbL-1.0, with an MIT license covering accompanying software packages, and describe community-driven updates, automated syncing, and real-time data validation as part of their maintenance process.

Open-source datasets in this space, most notably the countries-states-cities-database project, are distributed in multiple formats, including JSON, SQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, MongoDB, SQLite, XML, YAML, and CSV, and typically include ISO2 and ISO3 country codes, calling codes, capitals, native names, and time zone data, letting teams import the dataset directly into their own database rather than depending on a third-party API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Hosted API vs Self-Hosted Dataset
paragraphs: Teams evaluating this category generally choose between a hosted API, which offers convenience, a free tier, and no infrastructure to maintain, and a self-hosted open-source dataset, which offers full control over data freshness, uptime, and licensing at the cost of needing to run and update the microservice internally.

Hosted options are typically a better fit for smaller applications or teams that want to move quickly without managing infrastructure, while self-hosted datasets are more common in larger applications that need guaranteed uptime independent of a third-party provider, tighter data privacy controls, or the ability to customize or supplement the base dataset.

faqs.question: What is a Country-State-City Microservice API used for?
faqs.answer: It provides hierarchical country, state, and city reference data, typically used to power cascading address form fields, shipping and tax calculators, and location autocomplete components in applications.

faqs.question: Is Country-State-City API data free to use?
faqs.answer: Hosted services in this category, such as countrystatecity.in, offer a free tier to start building, and the underlying datasets, such as the countries-states-cities-database project, are also freely available as open source.

faqs.question: How much geographic data do these APIs typically cover?
faqs.answer: Leading implementations cover more than 250 countries, several thousand states or provinces, and well over 100,000 cities, though completeness at the city level varies by country.

faqs.question: Can I self-host a Country-State-City dataset instead of using a hosted API?
faqs.answer: Yes, open-source projects such as countries-states-cities-database distribute the dataset in formats including JSON, SQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, and CSV so it can be imported directly into a self-hosted database.

faqs.question: Do these APIs support GraphQL as well as REST?
faqs.answer: Some hosted implementations, including countrystatecity.in, expose data through both REST and GraphQL endpoints.

faqs.question: Do Country-State-City APIs include ISO country codes?
faqs.answer: Many implementations include ISO2 and ISO3 country codes, along with calling codes, capitals, native language names, and time zone data for countries.

faqs.question: Is there a single company that owns Country-State-City Microservice APIs?
faqs.answer: No, this is a category populated by multiple independent hosted services and open-source projects rather than a single owned product or company.

faqs.question: Can AI coding assistants use this kind of geographic data directly?
faqs.answer: Some newer implementations in this category ship as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, letting AI coding assistants and agents query country, state, and city data directly as part of a development workflow.
